findings.title: Shutdown frees sampler snapshots while log hot paths can still hold raw pointers
findings.impact: Log hot paths load `snapshot_` as a raw pointer and then dereference it without any reader lifetime guard. During shutdown, this line deletes the current snapshot immediately after an atomic exchange, so any concurrent LOG/PLOG/AccessRecorder path that already loaded the pointer can dereference freed memory and crash during worker or client teardown.
findings.fix: Do not reclaim snapshots until readers are quiesced. Publish disabled state before teardown and add a shutdown barrier for logging users, or replace the raw pointer scheme with a shared/RCU/hazard-pointer style lifetime so snapshots remain alive until all in-flight readers have left.

findings.title: Every sampler config change retains another snapshot until shutdown
findings.impact: Each distinct config update pushes the previous snapshot into `oldSnapshots_` and nothing frees those entries until process shutdown. A worker or client receiving repeated dynamic sampling updates will leak memory monotonically, which can eventually exhaust memory in a long-running service.
findings.fix: Use a bounded reclamation strategy tied to reader lifetime, such as shared snapshots or epoch/RCU reclamation, so old snapshots are freed after in-flight readers are safe instead of accumulating for the process lifetime.
